sections.title: Patterns with Threads
sections.content_summary: The section opens by drawing a parallel between nature's weavers — the baya weaver bird that builds hanging grass nests using an over-and-under technique — and human weaving. Weaving is explained as interlacing vertical and horizontal threads or strips to create a unified fabric such as cloth, mats, or baskets. The section notes that people have long woven natural materials including coconut fibre, palm reeds, bamboo, grass, jute, cotton, and silk. Indian muslin is highlighted as so fine it was known as 'woven air.'

tasks.task_text: Have you seen products woven out of natural material at home or elsewhere? What are they?

Take 5–6 strips of blue paper and tape them at the top of a surface. Take another set of yellow paper strips and weave them through—over, under, over, under. Keep repeating until you get a mat. Can you use this method to make a basket? Try using materials other than paper, such as strings, ropes, ribbons or reeds.
Look at a piece of cloth through a magnifying glass or by using zoom on a mobile phone camera. It could be a shirt or something you are wearing. Can you see the amazing criss-cross pattern?

sections.title: Traditions of Weaving
sections.content_summary: India's weaving history stretches back 4,000 years, with traditional cloth made on hand-operated looms — called handloom fabric. The section highlights India's rich regional handloom traditions: Kanjeevaram from Tamil Nadu, Pashmina from Kashmir, and Ikat from Odisha and Gujarat. Weaving is framed as both a cultural heritage and a livelihood for over 45 lakh people, especially women and rural artisans. Textile mills are contrasted as using modern machines to produce cloth in large quantities.

sections.title: Thread
sections.content_summary: This section traces how thread is made from raw fibres through spinning — twisting cotton fibres together using a charkha (spinning wheel) or, in the activity, a pencil. The individual fine strands are called fibres. Natural fibres (bamboo, cotton, linen, wool, silk) are distinguished from synthetic fibres (rayon, nylon, polyester, terylene). The silk moth's life cycle — eggs → caterpillar → cocoon → moth — is introduced to explain how silk thread is extracted. Gandhi ji's promotion of khadi through hand-spinning is highlighted as a symbol of self-sufficiency and the freedom struggle.

tasks.task_text: Take a ball of cotton and gently pull it out to make a strand. Now, try twisting the strand slowly with your fingers. Notice how it becomes stronger as you pull it in a spin. Take a pencil. Now, wind your cotton strand onto your pencil, by twisting and adding more cotton to your ball.

Look at some clothes, bags or other things you use every day. List some of the materials that you have used. Are they made from natural or synthetic fibres? Then, write one thing you like about it in the table below.

sections.title: Crafting with Needle and Thread
sections.content_summary: The tailorbird is introduced as nature's stitcher — it sews large leaves together using plant fibres or spider silk to create a nest-sleeve for its eggs. This parallels human stitching, where a needle and thread join cloth pieces. Students learn the basic running stitch (up-down-up-down in a straight line) and then use it to join cloth pieces, potentially building a patchwork table cloth, mat, or coaster. The section emphasises that stitching is a practical life skill — mending tears and sewing on buttons.

tasks.task_text: In small groups, collect fresh leaves of palash, teak, jackfruit or similar broad leaves. If leaves are not available, try using paper. Also, collect some small twigs like toothpicks. Now, using the leaves or pieces of paper and the toothpicks, pin them together to create a plate or a spoon.
Have you ever tried stitching? You will need a needle and thread to stitch a piece of fabric together. Can you fix a tear or sew a button? Let us learn simple stitching.

Let us begin by learning the basic running stitch. Take a piece of thread through a needle. Tie a knot at one end of the thread. Start from the back of the cloth. Bring the needle up at Point A. Push the needle down at Point B. Bring it up at Point C, then down at Point D. Keep going—up, down, up, down—in a straight line. This is called a running stitch.

Now, let us use this stitch to bring two pieces of cloth together. Collect small cloth pieces left over at a tailor's shop or some pieces of old cloth. Lay one piece of cloth flat on the table. Place the second piece of cloth on top of it, slightly overlapping it. Now, use a needle and thread to do a simple running stitch to join them together. Add more pieces to create a table cloth, mat, coaster, cleaning cloth or any material of your interest.

sections.title: Stitch and Decorate
sections.content_summary: The section presents India's diverse regional embroidery traditions — Chikankari (Lucknow), Banjara (Rajasthan), Kantha (West Bengal, Odisha, Tripura), Gota (Rajasthan), Phulkari (Punjab), Toda (Tamil Nadu), Kashmiri, and Khneng (Meghalaya) — showing that stitching is used not just to join fabric but to tell cultural stories through decorative patterns. Bandhani tie-dye from Rajasthan and Kala cotton from Gujarat are mentioned as other textile traditions. An Exhibition task asks students to display their handmade artefacts with labels.

sections.title: Recycle
sections.content_summary: India has a long tradition of not discarding old clothes — hand-me-downs to younger siblings, repurposing cloth into other items, and making quilts from small joined pieces. This section frames textile recycling as an embedded cultural practice rather than a modern trend. The Let us reflect tasks synthesise the whole chapter: revisiting weaving, stitching, recycling, the silkworm life cycle, and observing fabric properties of real cloth samples.

tasks.task_text: Have you ever reused or recycled an old piece of cloth? What did you or your family make from it?
If one thread breaks in a stitched cloth or in a woven mat, what might happen? Why is each thread important?
Visit a tailor's shop or a handloom store with an adult. What tools or machines did you see being used there?
Find out what kind of weaving or stitching work is famous in your area or state. Name it.
We should not throw the old clothes away. Why?

Below are the jumbled-up steps of the life cycle of a moth. Read and number them from 1 to 6 in the correct order. Adult moth comes out of the cocoon. Eggs hatch into tiny caterpillars. Silk moth lays eggs. The cycle begins again. Caterpillars eat mulberry leaves and grow big. Caterpillars spin cocoons around themselves.

Bring 5–6 pieces of different types of clothes from home or nearby tailors (leftover scraps). Observe the material closely and complete the table. Ask an elder or search in your book to find out whether it is made from cotton, wool, silk, jute, polyester or nylon.

dual_strand.natural: Natural fibres and their animal and plant sources — baya weaver bird, tailorbird, silkworm, cotton, wool, bamboo — illustrating how nature produces the raw material for cloth
dual_strand.human_cultural: India's handloom weaving traditions, spinning (charkha, khadi), regional embroideries, and the cultural practice of recycling and repurposing old cloth
